content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 5, 2006

Thomas J. Riggs & Thomas E. Gauthier
Lozano Smith – Attorneys At Law
7404 N. Spalding Avenue
Fresno, California 93720-3370

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-06-142

Dear Mr. Riggs and Gauthier:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Fresno's Chaffee Zoo Corporation regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Is Fresno's Chaffee Zoo Corporation ("Corporation") a "local government agency" for purposes of the Political Reform Act?

2. Is the Corporation required to adopt a conflict of interest code?

CONCLUSIONS

1. In the Siegel opinion, the Commission set forth criteria to consider in determining whether an entity should be considered a "local government agency" subject to the Act. Applying these criteria below, we conclude that the Corporation is a local government agency under section 82041 of the Act.

2. Because we have concluded that the Corporation is considered a local government agency under the Act, it is required to adopt a conflict of interest code under section 87300, or be included within a code.

_______________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 2

FACTS

Fresno Chaffee Zoo

You represent the Fresno Chaffee Zoo Corporation, a nonprofit corporation that
operates a regional zoo.

Prior to the formation of the Corporation, the zoo, which opened in the early
1900s, was known by various names: the Roeding Park Zoo; the Fresno Zoo; and the
Fresno Chaffee Zoo, commonly referred to as the Chaffee Zoo. Records are not clear
about the exact date, but the City of Fresno operated the zoo for the majority of its
existence, and the zoo employees were city employees.

Following several unsuccessful community-based attempts to establish a
permanent broad-based public financing mechanism for the Chaffee Zoo, members of the
community, including members of the Fresno Zoological Society ("Society")—a
nonprofit that supports and creates interest in zoo development—began informal
discussions which eventually led to the convening of a Blue Ribbon Chaffee Zoo
Committee consisting of 10 community members. No city, county, or other government
employees sat on the committee. The committee held a series of meetings to develop a
report and recommendations, which was presented to the Fresno City Council in 2004.
The Blue Ribbon Committee report contained a detailed strategic plan for growing the
Chaffee Zoo into a regional expanded zoo.

The recommendations included plans to: form a new 501(c)(3) organization with
financial support from throughout Fresno County; determine which parties would be
responsible for operations costs and maintenance when it took over zoo operations, and
establish a financing structure with proposals to have the City of Fresno pay $1.2 million
per year towards zoo operations and maintenance.

The Fresno City Council accepted the report and, by resolution, requested the
Fresno County Board of Supervisors to support the recommendations of the Blue Ribbon
Committee. The Fresno County Board of Supervisors agreed to place a tax measure on
the ballot to provide funding for the nonprofit Corporation which will be responsible for
the operations and maintenance of the zoo.

Voter Approval of Measure Z:

In November 2004, Fresno County voters approved the tax measure ("Measure
Z"), raising the sales tax rate by 0.10%. Measure Z, by its terms, will operate for ten
years, then the revenue source will expire. It is the Corporation's intention to become as
financially self-sufficient as possible during this ten-year window.

The sales tax would generate $8.2 million in revenue annually. Proceeds from the
sales tax increase go to the Fresno County Zoo Authority ("Authority"), a special purpose
government entity which will distribute the revenue. The Authority was created by

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 3

Fresno County Ordinance 2004-1. Under this ordinance, the Authority is charged with
distributing the sales tax revenues for purposes that support the Chaffee Zoo.

The Corporation and the Authority signed a revenue distribution agreement that
became effective on January 1, 2006. The Corporation will submit an operation and
maintenance budget, as well as a capital improvements budget. The Authority may
approve or disapprove these budgets, and the Corporation may change the budgets to
obtain the authority's approval. The Corporation will provide monthly financial reports,
and the Authority may inspect corporate books and records. Finally, the Corporation has
agreed to follow the tenets of the Brown Act, but "as a matter of sound public policy, not
as a matter of legal necessity."

Creation of Chaffee Zoo Corporation:

Following the approval of Measure Z, private citizens, including members of the
Society, created the new 501 (c)(3) corporation known as "The Chaffee Zoo
Corporation." The Society advanced all funds to retain a law firm to draw up
incorporation documents and to pay for other incorporation expenses. No government
entity paid any portion of the incorporation costs or participated in any way in the
formation of the Corporation.

The Corporation organized and held its first meetings in February and March of
2005. Until 2006, the Corporation received no public funds and operated solely on
private funds received from the Society.

However, the majority of funding for the Corporation for the near term will come
from Measure Z proceeds and the City of Fresno. Other funds will be generated from
gate receipts, donations, grants, fundraisers, special promotions, and other business
initiatives. Funds in excess of $1,000,000 will be transferred to the Corporation upon the
final dissolution of the Society.

The board of directors of the new Corporation consists of nine members. None
are City of Fresno or County of Fresno officials or employees. Of the original board
members, three were appointed by the city, three were appointed by the county, and three
were members of the Society. The Corporation used its own procedures in determining
the length of the terms for all members.

Since formation, three board members have resigned and a fourth board member's
term expired. The Corporation's board of directors interviewed and appointed
replacements without any input from the city, the county or the authority. All future
board appointments also will be made by the Corporation's board of directors.

It was the recommendation of the Blue Ribbon Committee that the Society
dissolves and contributes all of its assets net of liabilities, as well as its employees, to the
new Corporation. This process is nearly completed.

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 4

Corporation Lease with City of Fresno:

The Corporation has entered into a lease with the City of Fresno. The document contains terms typical of a commercial lease, plus terms specific to the on-going zoo operation. The initial term is for 30 years for $1 per year. However, if the tax revenue derived from Measure Z is extended or replaced with a substitute, the lease may be extended for 25 years. If the Measure Z tax revenue is not extended or replaced, the lease may be extended for successive 10-year terms, at the option of the City of Fresno.

The lease covers 39 acres of land, consisting of the "zoo premises" (18 acres) and an "expansion area" (21 acres). The city will retain possession of the expansion area until the Corporation desires to make capital improvements. Then the expansion area will become part of zoo premises.

The city retains ownership of the grounds, buildings, and permanent fixtures. The Corporation is responsible for operations and maintenance, and will pay for any additional improvements to the property. If the Corporation makes improvements to the zoo grounds, those improvements would become the city's property upon lease termination. Upon lease termination, all grounds, buildings, fixtures and personal property of the zoo will revert to city ownership.

The lease provides for no operational control or management by the city over zoo operations. The Corporation will submit financial statements to the city and promises to confine its operations to zoo purposes. The city, as landlord, would retain the right to approve improvements to the property.

The Corporation agreed to adhere to the "tenets" of the Brown Act.

Financing Agreement with City of Fresno:

In conjunction with the lease, the Corporation and the city have signed a financing agreement, effective January 1, 2006. It carries the same term as the lease, discussed above. It calls for the city to provide annual revenue to the Corporation in consideration for operating the zoo as follows: Year 1 - $1,200,000; Year 2 - $960,000; Year 3 - $720,000; Year 4 - $480,000, and Year 5 - $240,000.

The agreement also requires the city to convey title to zoo animals and zoo personal property such as vehicles, equipment, furniture, and computers. Finally, city employees who were working at the zoo may become employees of the Corporation. The Corporation may offer employment to desired employees, who may either accept the offer or transition into other available jobs with the City of Fresno. If city employees elect to transition back to other city jobs, the Corporation agrees to reimburse the city for salary and benefit costs until the transition is complete – no later than December 2006.

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 5

Current Status of Operations:

The lease and the financing agreement with the City of Fresno, and the agreement
regarding procedures for distribution of tax revenue with the Authority, became effective
on January 1, 2006. On that date, the Corporation took ownership of the zoo animals and
personal property and commenced operating the zoo. City employees who elected to
become employees of the Corporation have completed that transition. Employees of the
Society have transitioned to Corporation employment. A new chief executive officer for
the zoo has been hired by the Corporation. Other new employees for the zoo are being
recruited, interviewed and hired by the Corporation. The Corporation has applied for and
received its first allocation of Measure Z funds from the Zoo Authority. The Corporation
hosted an accreditation inspection team from the Association of Zoos and Aquariums and
is working with the association to assure reaccreditations of the zoo.

The board of directors of the Corporation has established a banking relationship
and commercial line of credit, has purchased new computers and telephone systems, and
is establishing a 401(k) deferred compensation program for its employees. The board has
approved job descriptions, salary schedules, vacation and sick leave policies, and expense
reimbursement protocols. The Corporation is represented by private legal counsel, and it
is in the process of retaining a private certified public accountant. The board has been
working with the Society to complete the dissolution of the Society and the transfer of the
Society's assets to the Corporation. That process is nearly complete. The board has
arranged for liability and property insurance and employee health benefit coverage. All
of these business activities have been undertaken by the corporate board.

ANALYSIS

I. Determining What Entities, Generally, Are Subject To The Act

The Act prohibits a public official from making or participating in making a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to know he or she has a
financial interest. (Section 87100.) These conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply
only to "public officials." A "public official" is defined as every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government agency. (Section 82048.)

In addition, section 87300 of the Act states that "[e]very agency shall adopt and
promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code" applicable to its "designated employees." For
the purposes of the Act, "agency" is interpreted to mean any state agency or local
government agency. (Section 82003; Maas Advice Letter, No. A-98-261.)

A "local government agency" is defined in the Act as "a county, city or district of
any kind including school district, or any other local or regional political subdivision, or
any department, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the
foregoing." (Section 82041.)

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 6

Your question is whether the Corporation is a local government agency. In two
opinions issued by the Commission nearly 30 years ago, the Commission used a four-part
factual test to determine when an organization is deemed to be an "agency" regulated
under the Act.

In the first opinion, In re Siegel, the Commission determined that the Pico Rivera
Water Development Corporation, a nonprofit corporation, was a local government agency
subject to the Act. (In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62.) The corporation was founded
to acquire, maintain, and operate a water system.

The Commission applied the following four-part test:

(1) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated with a
government agency;
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary source of
funds is, a government agency;
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the entity was formed is
to provide services or undertake obligations which public agencies are
legally authorized to perform and which, in fact, they traditionally have
performed; and
(4) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other laws.

In Siegel, the Commission concluded that: (1) the Pico Rivera City Council was
intimately involved in the formation of the nonprofit corporation, and the city council had
the right to disapprove anyone considered to serve on the board; (2) with respect to
funding, the city was required to pay rent to the corporation until the corporation's bonds
were retired, even if receipts from operation of the water system were not enough to meet
these costs—in essence guaranteeing the bonds; (3) with respect to principal purposes for
which the entity was formed, the Commission found that the acquisition and operation of
a water system is a service commonly provided by municipalities in their public
capacities; and (4) the corporation's bonds enjoyed the same legal status as those issued
by a public body under California's tax and securities laws. Thus, the Commission
concluded that the corporation was a local government agency and the members of the
corporation's board of directors were subject to the disclosure and disqualification
requirements of the Act.

However, the Siegel factors were not intended to be a definitive litmus test for
determining whether an entity is public for purposes of the Act. Ultimately, the test must
still be a factual analysis on a case-by-case basis. (In re Vonk (1981) 6 FPPC Ops. 1.)

The Commission's subsequent advice letters and an opinion state that it is not
necessary that all four of the Siegel factors be satisfied for an entity to be considered a
local government agency. (In re Vonk supra; O'Shea Advice Letter, No. A-91-570.) It is
only necessary that the entity satisfy enough of the four factors for its overall character to
correspond to that of a local government agency. (Rasiah Advice Letter, No. A-01-200.)

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 7

The Commission used the same four criteria to determine that the Bakersfield
Downtown Business Association and Chamber of Commerce were not "city agencies"
that were required to adopt a conflict of interest code. (In re Leach (1978) 4 FPPC Ops.
48.) In Leach, the Commission found that two nonprofit corporations were not
"agencies" under the Act because of contracts that they entered into with the City of
Bakersfield.

The Commission found that the primary purpose of the Downtown Business
Association and the Chamber of Commerce was nongovernmental in character. First, the
Downtown Business Association and Chamber of Commerce existed well before the
contracts at issue came into existence, and so the impetus for their creation was not
triggered by the city. Second, both entities were funded by private sources and only
received funds from the city necessary to reimburse the entities for their work under the
contracts. Third, even though the two entities engaged in business promotion activities
sometimes promoted by cities, the entities performed services which "more specifically
benefit the downtown business area and retail stores, restaurants, and hotels located
throughout the City" and therefore, could be construed to benefit private rather than
public interests. Fourth, although the entities enjoyed special tax status, neither was
viewed as public in nature by the tax laws.

The Commission stated that "although it is true that both the Association and the
Chamber perform certain functions for the City which presumably are beneficial to the
public, we do not think that these activities raise otherwise private entities to the level of
public agencies." (In re Leach, supra.)

The Siegel and Leach opinions both dealt with determining whether local entities
were public (governmental) or private (non-governmental) in character. In the Yonk
opinion, the Commission was faced with a different question – did the Act's conflict of
interest code provisions apply to the State Compensation Insurance Fund ("SCIF"), a
statewide agency that was created by the Legislature, but which functioned like a private
insurance company?

Despite the fact that SCIF's functions were similar to that of a private workmen's
compensation insurance company, the Commission concluded that the fund was a state
agency. The Yonk opinion stated:

"In Siegel and Leach we did isolate a number of specific criteria
which we thought helpful to determine whether ostensibly private entities
were truly public in nature. These criteria, however, were not intended to
be viewed as constituting a litmus test for determining whether an entity is
public for purposes of the Political Reform Act. Indeed, it seems to us that
criteria necessary to determine when private entities become so suffused
with attributes of sovereignty as to be considered public in nature, are
simply not necessary to determine whether an entity specifically
authorized by the state constitution is a public agency. In the case of

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 8

Fund, we believe its constitutional provenance makes it absolutely plain
that the Fund is public in nature." (Vonk, supra.)

II. Application Of The Siegel Criteria To The Corporation

Following these Commission opinions, we apply the Siegel test to the detailed
facts you have provided to determine whether the Corporation should be considered a
"local government agency" under the Act.

1. Did the impetus for formation of the entity originate with a government entity?

Generally, the first criterion is met where an entity is created by statute or
ordinance or by some official action of another governmental agency. (Maas Advice
Letter, No. A-98-261; Moser Advice Letter, No. A-97-400a.)

In this case, the idea for creating a nonprofit Corporation to operate the zoo was
formulated when members of the community (including members of the Society) began
discussions (with public officials) to create such an entity. These discussions led to the
creation of a Blue Ribbon Zoo Committee, made up of community members that
provided officials with a report and recommendations to create the nonprofit and to place
Measure Z, a sales tax measure, on the ballot to provide funding for the nonprofit's
operations. The Fresno City Council requested by resolution that the Fresno County
Board of Supervisors place the measure on the ballot, if the Society could get approval of
enabling legislation, which it did. You state in your letter that although "no officials of
the City or Fresno County participated significantly in this effort" to obtain the special
legislation to place the measure on the ballot, "Fresno County did report lobbying on
legislation, SB 1187."

Following voter approval of the ballot measure in 2004, members of the
community, including the Society, created the new 501(c)(3) Corporation, the Fresno
Chaffee Zoo Corporation. You state that no government entity paid any portion of the
incorporation costs or participated in any way in the formation of the Corporation.

The Authority, an independent nonprofit Corporation created by a Fresno County
ordinance, was formed to distribute the sales tax revenues from Measure Z to support the
Corporation's zoo operations. The Corporation and the Authority signed a revenue
distribution agreement to distribute the funds, which became effective on January 1,
2006.

The board of directors of the new Corporation consists of nine members, none of
which are officials or employees of the city or County of Fresno. Three of the original
board members were appointed by the city, three appointed by the county, and three were
members of the Society. However, since formation of the Corporation, three board
members resigned and a fourth board member's term expired. Your letter states that the
Corporation's board interviewed and appointed replacements for these members without
input from the city, county or the authority.

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 9

Effective January 1, 2006, were the lease and financing agreement between the
Corporation and the City of Fresno, and the procedures for distribution of tax revenue
with the Authority. The Corporation took ownership of the zoo animals and personal
property and commenced operating the zoo.

Returning to the first question in the Siegel analysis, here, the initial impulse for
the formation of the Corporation was from community members' desire to create a new
501(c)(3) nonprofit Corporation to take over zoo operations. However, the City of
Fresno and Fresno County were closely involved in the process by which the
responsibility for zoo operations was transferred from the City of Fresno to the
Corporation. Moreover, the formation of the Corporation would likely not have occurred
without the participation and support of the city and the county. The city approved a
lease and financing agreement with the Corporation and adopted a resolution urging the
Fresno County Board of Supervisors to place a county-wide sales tax measure on the
November 2004 ballot, which would finance zoo operations. The county lobbied for
approval of the special legislation in order to place the sales tax measure before the
voters, and created the Authority by ordinance to distribute the tax revenues to support
the Corporation. Therefore, the Siegel formation criterion is met.

2. Is the entity substantially funded by, or is its primary source of funds, a
government agency?

You note in your letter that "the Corporation meets criterion number 2" with
regard to funding. However, you also state in your letter that "it is the Corporation's
intention to become as financially self-sufficient as possible" during the next 10 years.
Currently, however, your facts indicate that the Corporation would be funded primarily
by the City of Fresno² and the county-wide sales tax revenue approved by voters through
Measure Z. Therefore, this criterion is also met.

3. Is one of the principal purposes for which the entity is formed, to provide
services or undertake obligations that public agencies are legally authorized to perform
and which, in fact, they traditionally have performed?

In the Siegel opinion, this third criterion is a two-part inquiry that examines
whether an entity performs a public function, and whether the service provided is one that
is traditionally performed by public agencies.

² Under the financing agreement between the City of Fresno and the Corporation, the city would
pay $1.2 million in 2006, during the first year of the financing agreement, and the amount would be
reduced by 20 percent each year over five years. The financing agreement also states that if sales tax
revenue ever ceased, the city's annual contribution to the Corporation would return to $1.2 million.
The agreement contemplates the Corporation receiving funding through the Authority, which would
distribute revenues from a county-wide tax measure which would be in effect for 10 years.

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 10

A. Public Function:

We first look at factors considered by the Siegel opinion to be relevant to
determining whether an entity performs a public function. One such factor is the degree
to which government actors control or are involved in its operations.

In the Siegel opinion, supra, the Commission noted that "further evidence that the
Corporation is fulfilling a public function under this plan is that the water system is to be
operated solely by city employees." In addition, the opinion looked at whether city
council members were members of the board of the nonprofit Corporation and considered
the fact that the city council had a right to disapprove the name of anyone submitted to
serve on the board. (In re Siegel, supra.)

According to your facts, the Corporation is an "independent contractor" whose
employees are not city employees. Furthermore, your facts indicate that with respect to
the Corporation's Board of Directors, no elected officials currently serve on its board,
and all future appointments will be made by the board without input from the city or
county.

However, your facts indicate that there is governmental control and involvement
in the Corporation's operations. One example is that six of the nine initial members of
the board were appointed by the city and county.³ In addition, it appears that the
Corporation's operation and maintenance costs, as well as its capital improvement plan,
are controlled by the Authority, "a political subdivision of the State of California."⁴

Thus, it appears that the Corporation is performing a public function, with
governmental entities—the city, county and the Authority - exerting control over the
Corporation's operations.

B. Service Traditionally Performed by Public Agencies:

Secondly, we look at factors considered by the Siegel opinion to be relevant in
determining whether an entity performs a function that has traditionally been performed
by public agencies.

³ Three of the original board members were appointed by the city, three appointed by the county,
and three were members of the Society. The initial term of the city's appointees are not specified in the
Corporation's Bylaws. However, one appointee of the county shall serve a one-year term, another a two-
year term and a third to serve a three-year term. The Zoological Society's appointees serve a one-year
term, a two-year term and a three-year term. The membership has changed since formation of the
Corporation because three board members resigned and a fourth board member's term expired. Your letter
does not specify which party or parties appointed these board members who resigned and whose term
expired.

⁴ The "Agreement Regarding Procedures for Distribution of Tax Revenue to the Chaffee Zoo," on
pages 2-4 states that the Authority has the power to "approve or disapprove the Corporation's budget" and
the Authority "may approve or disapprove the Corporation's capital improvement projects plan." In
addition, it states that, "the budget shall show that no more than one-third of the projected tax revenue will
be spent on operations and maintenance of the zoo."

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 11

According to information provided in your letter from the directory of the
Association of Zoos and Aquariums, 10 of the 16 zoos in California which are members
of the association are privately operated.

However, this number alone is not determinative, as member and non-member
zoos throughout the state are operated by both public and private entities. Furthermore,
your letter indicates that the zoo was founded in the early 1900s and that "City of Fresno
[has] operated the zoo for the majority of its existence." So based on your facts, it
appears that the operation of a zoo is a service that has traditionally been provided by the
city to the community. Thus, this factor is also met.

Under the facts presented, it appears that the Corporation, which is controlled by
governmental entities, is performing a public function and is providing a service that
government agencies traditionally perform.

4. Is the entity treated as a public entity by other statutory provisions?

The final factor which is considered in evaluating an entity's status under the Act
is whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other provisions of law.

Under federal tax law, the Corporation is treated as a nonprofit organization
exempt from taxation under section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. The
Corporation is incorporated in the State of California as a nonprofit Corporation.

You state in your letter that you are "not aware of other statutory [laws] which
would treat the Corporation as a public agency." You further state that the primary Acts
that could apply include the Brown Act and the Public Records Act. In addition, you
conclude that the Corporation, according to your analysis, is not subject to either the
Brown Act or the Public Records Act.

A copy of the bylaws of the Corporation, and the agreement between the
Authority and the Corporation to distribute tax revenue, state that the Corporation will
abide by the Brown Act and that the minutes of the meetings of the board of directors of
the Corporation shall be open to inspection by members of the public. However, the
bylaws and the agreement both state that the requirement was imposed as "a matter of
sound public policy and not as a legal necessity." You further state that, "[w]hile the
Corporation agreed in contract negotiations with the City and the Authority to follow the
tenets of the Brown Act, it expressly did so only as a matter of contract."

In prior advice, we have stated that a contractual provision requiring an entity to
abide by open meeting and public records laws means that the fourth prong of the Siegel
analysis is met. (Stark Advice Letter, No. A-03-015.) Therefore, based on the above
factors, the contractual provision requiring the Corporation to abide by open meetings
laws weighs in favor of meeting the fourth criterion. However, unlike the facts in the

File No. A-06-142
Page No. 12

Siegel opinion, in this case the fourth criterion adds little to the analysis, and is not
determinative of the Corporation's character as a governmental entity, or not.

Conclusion:

In applying the Siegel test to the facts presented in your letter, we find that all of
the four factors are met. Thus, we conclude that the Corporation is a local public agency
within the meaning of the Act, and will be required to have a conflict of interest code or
be included within a code pursuant to section 87300.5

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
ER:rd
I:\AdviceLetrs\06-142

_________________________
5 Please note that the Corporation will be subject to the provisions of section 87302.6, which
requires that all members of newly created agencies file statements of economic interests within 30 days of
assuming office. The statements must disclose all economic interests until a conflict of interest code with
more tailored disclosure categories is approved by the code reviewing body. (Section 87302.6; regulation
18754, enclosed.)

sections.question:
1. Is Fresno's Chaffee Zoo Corporation ("Corporation") a "local government
agenct'' for purposes of the Political Reform Act?
2. Is the Corporation required to adopt a conflict of interest code?
sections.conclusion:
1. In the Siegel opinion, the Commission set forth criteria to consider in
determining whether an entity should be considered a "local govemment
agenct''subject to the Act. Applying these criteria below, we conclude that
the Corporation is a local government agency under section 82041of the Act.
2. Because we have concluded that the Corporation is considered a local
government agency under the Act, it is required to adopt a conflict of interest
code under section 87300, or be included within a code.
I Govemment Code sections 8 1000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

government entity which will distribute the revenue. The Authority was created by
Fresno County Ordinance 2004-1. Under this ordinance, the Authority is charged with
distributing the sales tax revenues for purposes that support the ChaffeeZoo.
The Corporation and the Authority signed a revenue distribution agreement that
became effective on January 1,2006. The Corporation will submit an operation and
maintenance budget, as well as acapital improvements budget. The Authoitymay
approve or disapprove these budgets, and the Corporationmay change the budgets to
obtain the authority's approval. The Corporation will provide monthly financial reports,
and the Authority may inspect corporate books and records. Finally, the Corporation has
agreed to follow the tenets of the Brown Act, but o'as a matter of sound public policy, not
as a matter of legal necessity."
Creatton of Chaffee Zoo Corporation:
Following the approval of Measur e Z, pivate citizens, including members of the
Society, created the new 501 (c)(3) corporation known as "The Chaffee Zoo
Corporation." The Society advanced all funds to retain a law firm to draw up
incorporation documents and to pay for other incorporation expenses. No government
entity paid any portion of the incorporation costs or participated in any way in the
formation of the Corporation.
The Corporation organized and held its first meetings in February and March of
2005. Until2006, the Corporation received no public funds and operated solely on
private funds received from the Society.
However, the majority of funding for the Corporation for the near term will come
from Measure Zproceeds and the City of Fresno. Other funds will be generated from
gate receipts, donations, grants, fundraisers, special promotions, and other business
initiatives. Funds in excess of $1,000,000 will be transferred to the Corporation upon the
final dissolution of the Society.
The board of directors of the new Corporation consists of nine members. None
are City of Fresno or County of Fresno officials or ernployees. Of the original board
members, three were appointed by the city, three were appointed by the county, and three
were members of the Society. The Corporation used its own procedures in determining
the length of the terms for all members.
Since formation, three board members have resigned and a fourthboard member's
term expired. The Corporation's board of directors interviewed and appointed
replacements without any input from the city, the county or the authority. All future
board appointments also will be made by the Corporation's board of directors.
It was the recommendation of the Blue Ribbon Committee that the Society
dissolves and contributes all of its assets net of liabilities, as well as its ernployees, to the
new Corporation. This process is nearly completed.
Corporation Lease with City of Fresno:
The Corporation has entered into a lease with the City of Fresno. The document
contains terms typical of a commercial lease, plus terms specific to the on-going zoo
operation. The initial term is for 30 years for $1 per year. However, if the tax revenue
derived from Measure Z is extended or replaced with a substitute, the lease may be
extended for 25 years. If the Measure Z tax revenue is not extended or replaced, the lease
may be extended for successive l0-year terms, at the option of the City of Fresno.
The lease covers 39 acres of land, consisting of the "zoo premises" (18 acres) and
an "expansion area" (21 acres). The city will retain possession of the expansion area
until the Corporation desires to make capital improvements. Then the expansion area
will become part of zoo premises.
The city retains ownership of the grounds, buildings, and permanent fixtures. The
Corporation is responsible for operations and maintenance, and will pay for any
additional improvements to the property. If the Corporation makes improvements to the
zoo grounds, those improvements would become the city's property upon lease
termination. Upon lease termination, all grounds, buildings, fixtures and personal
property of the zoo will revert to city ownership.
The lease provides for no operational control or management by the city over zoo
operations. The Corporation will submit financial statements to the city and promises to
confine its operations to zoo purposes. The city, as landlord, would retain the right to
approve improvernents to the property.
The Corporation agreed to adhere to the "tenets" of the Brown Act.
Financing Agreement with City of Fresno:
In conjunction with the lease, the Corporation and the city have signed a financing
agreement, effective January 1,2006. It caries the same term as the lease, discussed
above. It calls for the city to provide annual revenue to the Corporation in consideration
for operatingthe zoo as follows: Year I - $1,200,000; Year 2 - $960,000; Year 3 -
$720,000; Year 4 - $480,000, and Year 5 - $240,000.
The agreement also requires the city to convey title to zoo animals and zoo
personal property such as vehicles, equipment, fumiture, and computers. Finally, city
employees who were working at the zoo may become employees of the Corporation. The
Corporation may offer employment to desired ernployees, who may either accept the
offer or transition into other available jobs with the City of Fresno. If city employees
elect to transition back to other city jobs, the Corporation agrees to reimburse the city for
salary and benefit costs until the transition is complete - no later than December 2006.
Current Status of Operations:
The lease and the financing agreement with the City of Fresno, and the agreement
regarding procedures for dishibution of tax revenue with the Authority, became effective
on January 1,2006. On that date, the Corporation took ownership of the zoo animals and
personal property and commenced operating the zoo. City employees who elected to
become employees of the Corporation have completed that transition. Employees of the
Society have transitioned to Corporation employment. A new chief executive officer for
the zoo has been hired by the Corporation. Other new employees for the zoo are being
recruited, interviewed and hired by the Corporation. The Corporation has applied for and
received its first allocation of Measure Z funds from the Zoo Authority. The Corporation
hosted an accreditation inspection team from the Association of Zoos and Aquariums and
is working with the association to assure reaccreditations of the zoo.
The board of directors of the Corporation has established a banking relationship
and commercial line of credit, has purchased new computers and telephone systems, and
is establishing a 401(k) deferred compensation program for its ernployees. The board has
approved job descriptions, salary schedules, vacation and sick leave policies, and expense
reimbursement protocols. The Corporation is represented by private legal counsel, and it
is in the process of retaining a private certified public accountant. The board has been
working with the Society to complete the dissolution of the Society and the transfer of the
Society's assets to the Corporation. That process is nearly complete. The board has
arranged for liability and property insurance and employee health benefit coverage. All
of these business activities have been undertaken by the corporate board.
AIIALYSIS
I. Determining What Entities, Generally, Are Subject To The Act
The Act prohibits a public official from making or participating in making a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to know he or she has a
financial interest. (Section 87100.) These conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply
only to "public officials." A "public official" is defined as everymember, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government agency. (Section 82048.)
In addition, section 87300 of the Act states that "[e]very agency shall adopt and
promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code" applicable to its "designated employees." For
the purposes of the Act, "agency'' is interpreted to mean any state agency or local
government agency. (Section 82003; Maas Advice Letter, No. 4-98-261.)
A "local govemment agenct''is defined in the Act as "a county, city or district of
any kind including school district, or any other local or regional political subdivision, or
any departrnent, division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the
foregoing." (Section 82041.)
Your question is whether the Corporation is a local government agerrcy. In two
opinions issued by the Commission nearly 30 years ago, the Commission used a four-part
facfual test to determine when anorganization is deemed to be an "agenct''regulated
under the Act.
In the first opinion,In re Siegel, the Commission determined that the Pico Rivera
Water Development Corporation, a nonprofit corporation, was a local govemment agency
subject to the Acl (In re Siegel (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 62.) The corporation was founded
to acquire, maintain, and operate awater system.
The Commission applied the following four-part test:
(l) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated with a
government agency;
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary source of
funds is, a govemment agency;
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the entity was formed is
to provide services or undertake obligations which public agencies are
legally authorized to perform and which, infact,they traditionallyhave
performed; and
(4) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other laws.
In Siegel, the Commission concluded that: (1) the Pico Rivera City Council was
intimately involved in the formation of the nonprofit corporation, and the city council had
the right to disapprove anyone considered to serve on the board; (2) withrespect to
funding, the city was required to pay rent to the corporation until the corporation's bonds
were retired, even if receipts from operation of the water system were not enough to meet
these costs-in essence guaranteeing the bonds; (3) with respect to principal purposes for
which the entity was formed, the Commission found that the acquisition and operation of
a water system is a service commonly provided by municipalities in their public
capacities; and (a) the corporation's bonds enjoyed the same legal status as those issued
by apublic bodyunder California's tax and securities laws. Thus, the Commission
concluded that the corporation was a local government agency and the rnembers of the
corporation's board of directors were subject to the disclosure and disqualification
requirements of the Act.
However, the Siegel factors were not intended to be a definitive litmus test for
determining whether an entity is public for purposes of the Act. Ultimately, the test must
still be a factual analysis on a case-by-case basis. (In re Yonk (1981) 6 FPPC Ops. 1.)
The Commission's subsequent advice letters and an opinion state that it is not
necessary that all four ofthe Siegel factors be satisfied for an entity to be considered a
local government agency. (In re Vonk supra; O'Shea Advice Letter, No. A-91-570.) It is
only necessary that the entity satisff enough of the four factors for its overall character to
correspond to that of a local government agency. (Rasiah Advice Letter, No. A-01-020.)
The Commission used the same four criteria to determinethatthe Bakersfield
Downtown Business Association and Chamber of Comrnerce were not "city agencies"
that were required to adopt a conflict of interest code. (In re Leach (1978) 4 FPPC Ops.
48.) In Leach, the Commission found that two nonprofit corporations were not
"agencies" under the Act because of contracts that they entered into with the City of
Bakersfield.
The Commission found that the primary pu{pose of the Downtown Business
Association and the Chamber of Commerce was nongovemmental in character. First, the
Downtown Business Association and Chamber of Commerce existed well before the
contracts at issue came into existence, and so the impetus for their creation was not
tiggeredby the city. Second, both entities were funded by private sources and only
received funds from the city necessary to reimburse the entities for their work under the
contracts. Third, even though the two entities engaged in business prornotion activities
sometimes conducted by cities, the entities performed services which "more specifically
benefit the downtown business area and retail stores, restaurants, and hotels located
throughout the City''and therefore, could be construed to benefit private rather than
public interests. Fourth, although the entities enjoyed special tax status, neither was
viewed as public in nature by the tax laws.
The Commission stated that "although it is true that both the Association and the
Chamber perform certain functions for the City which presumably are beneficial to the
public, we do not think that these activities raise otherwise private entities to the level of
public agencies." Qn re Leach, supra.)
The Siegel and Leach opinions both dealt with determining whether local entities
were public (governmental) or private (non-governmental) in character. Inthe Yonk
opinion, the Commission was faced with a different question - did the Act's conflict of
interest code provisions apply to the State Compensation Insurance Fund ("SCIF'), a
statewide agency that was created by the Legislature, but which functioned like a private
insurance company?
Despite the fact that SCIF's functions were similar to that of a private workmen's
compensation insurance company, the Commission concluded that the fund was a state
agency. The Vonk opinion stated:
"In Siegel and Leach we did isolate a number of specific criteria
which we thought helpful to determine whether ostensiblyprivate entities
were truly public in nature. These criteria, however, were not intended to
be viewed as constituting a litmus test for determining whether an entity is
public for purposes of the Political Reform Act. Indeed, it seems to us that
criteria necessary to determine when private entities become so suffused
with attributes of sovereignty as to be considered public in nature, are
simplynot necessary to determine whether an entity specifically
authorized by the state constitution is a public agency. In the case of


FileNo. A-06-142
Fund, we believe its constitutional provenance makes it absolutely plain
that the Fund is public in nafure." (Vonk, supra.)
II. Application Of The Siegel Criteria To The Corporation
Following these Commission opinions, we apply the Siegel test to the detailed
facts you have provided to determine whether the Corporation should be considered a
"local government agenct''under the Act.
1. Did the impetus forformation of the entity originate with a government entity?
Generally, the first criterion is met where an entity is created by statute or
ordinance or by some official action of another governmental agency. (Maas Advice
Letter,No. A-98-261; Moser Advice Letter, No. A-97-400a.)
In this case, the idea for creating a nonprofit Corporation to operate the zoo was
formulated when members of the community (including mernbers ofthe Society) began
discussions (with public officials) to create such an entity. These discussions led to the
creation of a Blue Ribbon Zoo Committee, made up of community members that
provided officials with a report and recommendations to create the nonprofit and to place
Measure Z, a sales tax measure, on the ballot to provide funding for the nonprofit's
operations. The Fresno City Council requested by resolution that the Fresno County
Board of Supervisors place the measure on the ballot, if the Society could get approval of
enabling legislation, which it did. You state in your letter that although "no officials of
the City or Fresno County participated significantly in this effort" to obtain the special
legislation to place the measure on the ballot, "Fresno County did report lobbying on
legislation, SB 1187."
Following voter approval of the ballot measure in2004, members of the
community, including the Society, created the new 501(c)(3) Corporation, the Fresno
Chaffee Zoo Corporation. You state that no govemment entity paid any portion of the
incorporation costs or participated in any way in the formation of the Corporation.
The Authoity, an independent nonprofit Corporation created by a Fresno County
ordinance, was formed to distribute the sales tax revenues from Measure Z to support the
Corporation's zoo operations. The Corporation and the Authority signed a revenue
diskibution agreement to distribute the funds, which became effective on January l,
2006.
The board of directors of the new Corporation consists of nine members, none of
which are officials or employees of the city or County of Fresno. Three of the original
board members were appointed by the city, three appointed by the countS and three were
members of the Society. However, since formation of the Corporation, three board
members resigned and a fourth board member's term expired. Your letter states that the
Corporation's board interviewed and appointed replacements for these members without
input from the city, county or the authority.
Effective January 1,2006, were the lease and financing agreement between the
Corporation and the City of Fresno, and the procedures for distribution of tax revenue
with the Authority. The Corporation took ownership of the zoo animals and personal
property and commenced operatingthe zoo.
Returning to the first question in the Siegel analysis, here, the initial impulse for
the formation of the Corporation was from community members' desire to create a new
501(c)(3) nonprofit Corporation to take over zoo operations. However, the City of
Fresno and Fresno County were closely involved in the process by which the
responsibility for zoo operations was transferred from the City of Fresno to the
Corporation. Moreover, the formation of the Corporation would likely not have occurred
without the participation and support of the city and the county. The city approved a
lease and financing agreement with the Corporation and adopted a resolution urging the
Fresno County Board of Supervisors to place a county-wide sales tax measure on the
November 2004ballot, which would finance zoo operations. The county lobbied for
approval of the special legislation in order to place the sales tax measure before the
voters, and created the Authority by ordinance to distribute the tax revenues to support
the Corporation. Therefore, the Siegel formation criterion is met.
2. Is the entity substantiallyfunded by, or is its primary source offunds, a
government agency?
You note in your letter that "the Corporation meets criterion number 2" with
regard to funding. However, you also state in your letter that*it is the Corporation's
intention to become as financially self-sufficient as possible" during the next l0 years.
Currently, however, your facts indicate that the Corporation would be funded primarily
by the City of Fresno2 and the county-wide sales tai revenue approved by voters through
Measure Z. Therefore, this criterion is also met.
3. Is one of the principal purposes for which the entity is formed, to provide
services or undertake obligations that public agencies are legaily authorized to perform
and which, infact, they traditionally have pedormed?
In the Siegel opinion, this third criterion is a two-part inquiry that examines
whether an entity performs a public function, and whether the service provided is one that
is traditionally performed by public agencies.
2 Under the financing agreement between the City of Fresno and the Corporation, the city would
pay $1.2 million ri,2006, during the fust year of the financing agreement, and the amount would be
reduced by 20 percent each year over five years. The financing agreement also states that ifsales tax
revenue ever ceased, the city's annual contribution to the Corporation would return to S1.2 million.
The agreement contemplates the Corporation receiving funding through the Authority, which would
dishibute revenues from a county-wide tax measure which would be in effect for l0 years.
A. Public Function:
We first look at factors considered by the Siegel opinion to be relevant to
determining whether an entityperforms apublic function. One such factor is the degree
to which government actors control or are involved in its operations.
In the Siegel opinion, supra, the Commission noted that "further evidence that the
Corporation is fulfilling a public function under this plan is that the water system is to be
operated solely by city employees." In addition, the opinion looked at whether city
council members were members of the board of the nonprofit Corporation and considered
the fact that the city council had a right to disapprove the name of anyone submitted to
serve on the board. (In re Siegel, supra.)
According to your facts, the Corporation is an "independent contractor" whose
employees are not city employees. Furthennore, your facts indicate that with respect to
the Corporation's Board of Directors, no elected officials currently serye on its board,
and all future appointments will be made by the board without input from the city or
county.
However, your facts indicate that there is governmental control and involvernent
in the Corporation's operations. One example is that six of the nine initial members of
the board were appointed by the city and county.3 In addition, it appears that the
Corporation's operation and maintenance costs, as well as its capital improvement plan,
are controlled by the Authority,o'apolitical subdivision of the State of California."a
Thus, it appears that the Corporation is performing a public function, with
govemmental entities-the city, county and the Authority - exerting control over the
Corporation's operations.
B. Service Traditionally Performed by Public Agencies:
Secondly, we look at factors considered by the Siegel opinion to be relevant in
determining whether an entity performs a function that has traditionally been performed
by public agencies.
3 Three of the original board members were appointed by the city, tlree appointed by the county,
and three were members of the Society. The initial term of the city's appointees are not specified in
Corporation's Bylaws. However, one appointee of the county shall serve a one-year temr, another a two-
year term and a third to serve a three-year term. The Zoological Society's appointees serve a one-year
term, a two-year term and a three-year term. The membership has changed since formation of the
Corporation because three board members resigned and a fourth board member's term expired. Your letter
does not speciSr which party or parties appointed these board members who resigned and whose term
expired.
o The "Agreement Regarding Procedures for Distribution of Tax Revenue to the Chaffe e Zoo," ol
pages 2-4 states that the Authority has the power to "approve or disapprove the Corporation's budget" and
the Authority "may approve or disapprove the Corporation's capital improvement projects plan." In
addition, it states that, "The budget shall show that no more than one-third of the projected tax revenue will
be spent on operations and maintenance of the zoo."
According to information provided in your leffer from the directory of the
Association of Zoos and Aquariums, 10 of the 16 zoos in Califomia which are members
of the association arepivately operated.
However, this number alone is not determinative, as member and non-member
zoos throughout the state are operated by both public and private entities. Furthermore,
your letter indicates that the zoo was founded in the early 1900s and that *City of Fresno
[has] operated the zoo for the majority of its existence." So based on your facts, it
appears that the operation of a zoo is a service that has traditionally been provided by the
city to the community. Thus, this factor is also met.
Under the facts presented, it appears that the Corporation, which is controlled by
governmental entities, is performing a public function and is providing a service that
government agencies traditionally perform.
4. Is the entity treated as a public entity by other statutory provisions?
The final factor which is considered in evaluating an entity's status under the Act
is whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other provisions of law.
Under federal taxlaw, the Corporation is treated as a nonprofit organization
exernpt from taxation under section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. The
Corporation is incorporated in the State of California as a nonprofit Corporation.
You state in your letter that you are "not aware of other statutory [aws] which
would treat the Corporation as a public agency." You further state that the primary Acts
that could apply include the Brown Act and the Public Records Act. In addition, you
conclude that the Corporation, according to your analysis, is not subject to either the
Brown Act or the Public Records Act.
A copy of the bylaws of the Corporation, and the agreement between the
Authority and the Corporation to distribute tax revenue, state that the Corporation will
abide by the Brown Act and that the minutes of the meetings of the board of directors of
the Corporation shall be open to inspection by members of the public. However, the
bylaws and the agreement both state that the requirement was imposed as "a matter of
sound public policy and not as a legal necessity." You further state that, "[w]hile the
Corporation agreed in contract negotiations with the City and the Authority to follow the
tenets of the Brown Act, it expressly did so only as a matter of contract."
In prior advice, we have stated that a contractual provision requiring an entity to
abide by open meeting and public records laws means that the fourth prong of the Siegel
analysis is met. (Stark Advice Letter, No. A-03-015.) Therefore, based on the above
factors, the contractual provision requiring the Corporation to abide by open meetings
laws weighs in favor of meeting the fourth criterion. However, unlike the facts in the
Siegel opinion, in this case the fourth criterion adds little to the analysis, and is not
determinative of the corporation's character as a government al entiiy,or not.
Conclusion:
In applying the Siegel test to the facts presented in your letter, we find that all of
the four factors aremet. Thus, we conclude that the Corpoiation is alocalpublic agerLcy
within the meaning of the Act, and will be required to have a conflict of inGrest code or
be included within a code pursuant to section g73OO.s

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Is Fresno's Chaffee Zoo Corporation ("Corporation") a "local government
agenct'' for purposes of the Political Reform Act?
2. Is the Corporation required to adopt a conflict of interest code?

CONCLUSION: 1. In the Siegel opinion, the Commission set forth criteria to consider in
determining whether an entity should be considered a "local govemment
agenct''subject to the Act. Applying these criteria below, we conclude that
the Corporation is a local government agency under section 82041of the Act.
2. Because we have concluded that the Corporation is considered a local
government agency under the Act, it is required to adopt a conflict of interest
code under section 87300, or be included within a code.
I Govemment Code sections 8 1000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 5, 2006 Thomas J. Riggs & Thomas E. Gauthier Lozano Smith – Attorneys At Law 7404 N. Spalding Avenue Fresno, California 93720-3370 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-06-142 Dear Mr. Riggs and Gauthier: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Fresno's Chaffee Zoo Corporation regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. Is Fresno's Chaffee Zoo Corporation ("Corporation") a "local government agency" for purposes of the Political Reform Act? 2. Is the Corporation required to adopt a conflict of interest code? CONCLUSIONS 1. In the Siegel opinion, the Commission set forth criteria to consider in determining whether an entity should be considered a "local government agency" subject to the Act. Applying these criteria below, we conclude that the Corporation is a local government agency under section 82041 of the Act. 2. Because we have concluded that the Corporation is considered a local government agency under the Act, it is required to adopt a conflict of interest code under section 87300, or be included within a code. _______________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-06-142 Page No. 2 FACTS Fresno Chaffee Zoo You represent the Fresno Chaffee Zoo Corporation, a nonprofit corporation that operates a regional zoo. Prior to the formation of the Corporation, the zoo, which opened in the early 1900s, was known by various names: the Roeding Park Zoo; the Fresno Zoo; and the Fresno Chaffee Zoo, commonly referred to as the Chaffee Zoo. Records are not clear about the exact date, but the City of Fresno operated the zoo for the majority of its existence, and the zoo employees were city employees. Following several unsuccessful community-based attempts to establish a permanent broad-based public financing mechanism for the Chaffee Zoo, members of the community, including members of the Fresno Zoological Society ("Society")—a nonprofit that supports and creates interest in zoo development—began informal discussions which eventually led to the convening of a Blue Ribbon Chaffee Zoo Committee consisting of 10 community members. No city, county, or other government employees sat on the committee. The committee held a series of meetings to develop a report and recommendations, which was presented to the Fresno City Council in 2004. The Blue Ribbon Committee report contained a detailed strategic plan for growing the Chaffee Zoo into a regional expanded zoo. The recommendations included plans to: form a new 501(c)(3) organization with financial support from throughout Fresno County; determine which parties would be responsible for operations costs and maintenance when it took over zoo operations, and establish a financing structure with proposals to have the City of Fresno pay $1.2 million per year towards zoo operations and maintenance. The Fresno City Council accepted the report and, by resolution, requested the Fresno
